dsls.markdown:
# Teaching and Learning CS Discoveries #### Welcome to Module 4! In this module, you will be introduced to authentic CS practices that students will engage with throughout the CS Discoveries course. These practices include debugging, using resources, and pair programming. You will also explore the role of the teacher within a CS Discoveries classroom and make a plan for how you will support student learning while teaching the course. ### **You will:** * #### Consider the role of the teacher in a CS Discoveries classroom. * #### Learn about authentic CS practices that students will develop throughout the CS Discoveries course. * #### Make a plan for how you will support student learning through differentiation, ongoing assessment, and promoting a culture of inclusion within your CS classroom. #### You will begin with a lesson focused on the role of the teacher in a CS Discoveries classroom.
#### **Suggested time:** 50 minutes

# Role of the Teacher ## What is my role as the teacher in a CS Discoveries classroom? #### CS Discovers teachers are active participants in the learning experience. Teachers are responsible for fostering an inclusive growth-oriented culture, modeling good learning strategies, and supporting students in developing the target skills and understandings. In a CS Discoveries classroom, the teacher is the lead learner who guides students in solving their own problems, developing new skills, and gaining new knowledge.

As the lead learner, your role shifts from being the source of knowledge to being a leader in seeking knowledge. Lead learners use modeling frequently and use metacognition, thinking about your thinking, often.


## How can I be a lead learner for my students? * #### Allow students to dive into an activity without front-loading the content first. * #### Encourage students to rely on each other for support. * #### Don't answer right away, even if you know the answer. * #### Feel open to making mistakes in front of students. Mistakes are part of the learning process. * #### Ask students questions that direct their attention toward the issue to investigate without giving away what they need to change. * #### Model the steps you would go through as a learner of a new subject. Explain the different questions you ask yourself along the way and the ways you go about finding answers. ## How is being a lead learner connected to the curriculum? #### Acting as the lead learner is an act of empathy toward your students and the challenges they face in learning material for the first time. A critical job in the CS Discoveries classroom is to model excitement about investigating how things work by asking motivating questions about why things work the way they do and why they are the way they are. With your guidance, students will learn how to hypothesize, ask peers, test, evaluate, and refine solutions collaboratively. A teacher who embraces the lead learner role can lead the course without knowing all of the answers.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
## Consider the following when watching this video:

#### What will be most challenging for you as you take on the lead learner role? What will you do to overcome this challenge?

dsls.markdown:
# Learning CS Discoveries

#### An important role of the teacher in the CS Discoveries classroom is to support students in developing authentic CS practices as they learn new programming skills and build new CS content knowledge. 

### **You will:**

* #### Learn about authentic CS practices that students will develop throughout the CS Discoveries course.

# Pair Programming

Pair programming is a technique in which two programmers work together at one computer. One, the driver, writes code while the other, the navigator, directs the driver on the design and setup of the code. The two programmers switch roles often.


## What is my role as the teacher? #### As the teacher, you play a critical role in determining and establishing routines for how your students will use the pair programming strategy. ### Pair programming has been shown to: * #### Improve computer science enrollment, retention, and students' performance. * #### Increase students' confidence. * #### Develop students' critical thinking skills. * #### Introduce students to the “real world” working environment.
🤝🏽 How do I begin pair programming with my students?

1. #### Form pairs. 2. #### Give each pair one computer. 3. #### Decide upon initial roles (driver or navigator). 4. #### Have students start working. 5. #### Ensure that students switch roles at regular intervals (every 3 to 5 minutes). 6. #### Ensure that the navigators remain active participants.

🔎 How is pair programming connected to the curriculum?

#### In CS Discoveries, there are many lessons on the computer which students develop programming skills. Pair programming can foster a sense of camaraderie and collaboration in the classroom. In addition, pair programming can promote inclusion by reducing the "confidence gap" between female and male students while increasing all students' programming confidence.

📝 Additional Resources

* #### The ETR Pair Programming Toolkit provides additional strategies for implementing Pair Programming in your classroom. Check it out at: http://bit.ly/pair-programming-toolkit * #### The National Center for Women & Information Technology (NCWIT) has a great resource about the benefits of pair programming. Check it out at: http://bit.ly/pair-programming-ncwit

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
#### This video is included in the CS Discoveries curriculum to introduce students to pair programming.

## As you watch the video, consider the following:

### How might my students benefit from pair programming?

You will be able to track both team progress and individual student progress from your teacher dashboard. Levels completed using the pair programming feature will have a people icon in the center of the completed level bubble.


Note: Code.org is rolling out a new version of the progress view! While the new progress view is being rolled out, you will still have access to the old one. Please note that the above view for pair programming is not currently set up in the new progress view.

dsls.markdown:
# Using Resources

Using resources is a core, authentic practice of computer science. Whether novice or expert, all programmers rely on code documentation and other resources when writing programs.

## What is my role as the teacher? #### Your role as the teacher is to help students develop the skill of finding answers on their own, rather than directly solving their problems for them.
❓ How is using resources related to the problem solving process?

* #### **Define** - Describe what you need to know. * #### **Prepare** - Decide where to look for the information. * #### **Try** - Read the resources. * #### **Reflect** - Think about what worked.

🧰 What resources are available to students? #### CS Discoveries includes several key resources to support students in the learning process. For the most part, these resources are set up to be used as needed, not read as a pre-reading or presented as a lecture. Students are not expected to already know all of the needed information when they engage in programming activities. This structure reflects the “discovery” model of the course, but also supports students in the authentic use of resources and code documentation.
🎞️ Videos

#### **Videos** provide direct instruction on certain topics throughout the curriculum. Relevant videos are embedded within the **“Help and Tips”** tab, so that students can access them at any time in the online progression. #### To read more about how to effectively use our curriculum videos, see our Guide to Curriculum Videos

📝 Reference Materials

#### **Reference materials** are standalone resources that provide information on particular concepts of programming structures. Relevant resources are found in the **"Help and Tips"** tab in Code Studio levels. They can be used as resources or review when students are working, or as a format for individual or group sensemaking after students have completed a learning activity.

💻 Code Documentation

#### **Code Documentation** provides extra documentation on particular blocks or commands. Students can access these directly by hovering over blocks or browse through the block index on the documentation pages.

🔎 How is using resources connected to the curriculum?

#### Informational resources are available to students throughout programming lessons to give them just-in-time information to solve their problems. These resources allow students to self-differentiate, deciding for themselves how much help they need for a particular activity. Students should continue to explore resources and previous solutions throughout the lessons as they experiment and create computational artifacts.

### Click here to read the CS Discoveries Guide to Using Resources.

A big part of using Game Lab is understanding position. Remember you can always turn on the grid or hover with the mouse to help find the x and y position you want.

# Do This

* Hover over the block in the toolbox, then click "See Examples" to open the code documentation.
* When you are finished exploring, click the orange "Run" under the preview section on the left hand side of the screen. Then click the "Finish" button.

dsls.markdown:
# Debugging

Debugging is the act of finding and fixing problems in code.

## What is my role as the teacher? #### Your role as the teacher is to avoid directly debugging for your students, and instead, to guide them in the development of their own debugging skills.
#### **Teacher Tips:** * #### Ask questions about the code (and what changes were made when the bug was introduced), making sure that the students can clearly explain how the code is intended to work. * #### Have students read their code aloud, line by line, explaining the purpose of each command. * #### Avoid finding the bug for students or being too specific with your questioning. * #### Celebrate discovering (and fixing) new types of bugs to normalize the debugging process.
🐛 How is debugging connected to the curriculum?

#### Occasionally students will encounter a debugging level where they are explicitly asked to identify and fix bugs in provided code. While these are great opportunities to highlight specific kinds of errors and misconceptions, it's important to build a culture of constant debugging, as this isn't an activity that is done in isolated moments.


#### Click here to read the CS Discoveries Guide to Debugging.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
#### This video is included in the CS Discoveries curriculum to introduce students to debugging.

## As you watch the video, consider the following:

### How can I normalize the idea for my students that "everyone gets bugs!"?

dsls.markdown: # Task: Locate Additional Debugging Support ## What do I do if my student finds a bug and I don't know how to help them? #### **Step 1:** Open a new tab and log into your teacher account on Code.org. #### **Step 2:** Click on the "?" in the upper right hand corner of your screen. #### **Step 3:** Select "Teacher forum" from the dropdown menu. #### **Step 4:** Select the "CS Discoveries" category from the teacher forum homepage. #### **Step 5:** Select the "Coding and Debugging Help" category. #### **Step 6:** Read the post titled "Tips for getting help quickly". #### **Step 7:** Click on two or three topics within this category to get a feel for the types of questions people are asking and the responses they are receiving.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
## Consider the following when watching this video:

#### In your role as the teacher, what can do to support students through the process of debugging?

Collaborative problem solving is an important part of pair programming, using resources, and debugging. The teacher plays an important role in establishing a classroom culture that promotes and celebrates collaborative problem solving.

#### Use the space below to document your plan for developing a classroom culture that promotes and celebrates collaborative problem solving. Consider the following when making your plan: * #### What culture do you hope to create in your classroom? * #### What steps can you, as the teacher, take to promote and sustain the development of that culture? * #### What routines and procedures will you put in place to support students in pair programming, using resources, and debugging?
dsls.title: Make a Plan: Supporting the Development of Authentic CS Practices

dsls.markdown:
# Teaching CS Discoveries 

#### The teacher plays an active role in developing the classroom culture and supporting learning in the CS Discoveries classroom. In this lesson you will consider the role of the teacher in inclusion, differentiation, and assessment. 

### **You will learn how to:**

* #### Make a plan for how you will support student learning through differentiation, ongoing assessment, and promoting a culture of inclusion within your CS classroom.

# Inclusion

Inclusion is creating learning environments as well as using curricula and resources that are accessible, welcoming, and reflective of all students' identities, backgrounds, differences, and perspectives.

## What is my role as the teacher? #### Your role as the teacher is to create a culture within your classroom where all students regardless of race, gender, language, ability, or socioeconomic background feel like they belong.
🔎 How does the curriculum support inclusion?

#### The CS Discoveries curriculum is intentionally designed to avoid privileging prior knowledge while honoring student voice and choice. This shows up in the curriculum in the following ways:
* #### The course begins with a unit focused on the problem solving process instead of jumping straight into programming. * #### Warm up prompts provide students the opportunity to bring in outside experiences. * #### Lessons include scaffolding to support new to CS learners. * #### Programming lessons include choice levels that provide additional practice or challenge based on students' needs. * #### Videos include a diverse set of voices so students can see themselves represented in CS roles. * #### Projects incorporate student choice and opportunities for self-expression. * #### An emphasis is placed on students using resources to solve problems.

🧑🏻‍🤝‍🧑🏿 What can I do to promote a culture of inclusion in my classroom?

Be a lead learner #### Students will encounter bugs in their code and run into problems that are challenging to solve. Normalizing failure and promoting a growth mindset within your classroom will help students know how to respond to and overcome these challenges.
Provide multiple opportunities for students to showcase their knowledge #### Students will likely excel in different ways. Providing multiples ways for students to showcase their knowledge creates new opportunities for students to feel successful in CS.
Consider your classroom environment #### What story does your classroom tell about who belongs in CS? Do all students see themselves represented in the images on your walls? Do the projects displayed on your walls feel approachable to new to CS students? Are there aspects of your classroom that promote a growth mindset?
Pay attention to student groupings #### The curriculum includes many opportunities for students to collaborate and work together. Student groupings can have a big impact on whether or not a student feels like they belong in CS. Consider student personalities, prior knowledge, and needs when selecting groups of students to work together.
Incorporate multiple student voices into class discussions #### When students are engaging in whole group discussions, look for ways to draw in participation from students who might be more hesitant to contribute. You may even choose to highlight examples of something you observed during a time when students were working independently or in groups.

## Additional Resource #### The Microsoft Guide to Inclusive CS Education is a great resource for learning more about access, diversity, and inclusion in CS education. Click here to read the guide.

answers.text: High achieving students who are looking for an additional academic challenge
Students with computer science prior knowledge and experience
All students regardless of race, gender, language, ability, or socioeconomic background
All students can participate, but the course was designed for students with prior CS knowledge.

dsls.markdown: ![](https://images.code.org/c6a02a8487c97a7c91abb0268c52fa94-image-1625772235783.png)

questions.text: Who is CS Discoveries designed for?

dsls.title: Check for Understanding

dsls.markdown:
#### Teachers play a role in ensuring all students feel like they belong in a class. Use the space below to document your plan for creating a classroom culture where all students feel like they belong. 

### **Consider the following when making your plan:**

* #### What potential threats to inclusion exist in your classroom?
* #### What steps will you take to make sure all students feel like they belong in your CS classroom?
* #### How will you know if you are successful in creating an inclusive classroom?
dsls.title: Make a Plan: Supporting Inclusion

dsls.markdown:
# Differentiation

Your role as the teacher is to ensure that all students are able to access the course content in a way that meets their unique learning needs. This might mean modifying the lesson plan for the whole class, modifying activities for individual students, or other modifications necessary to support learning within your classroom.

## Three Ways to Differentiate Lessons: * #### **Differentiate by task:** Differentiated tasks allow students to practice a new skill or demonstrate mastery of a concept while providing flexibility in the product or program they create. * #### **Differentiate by timing:** For some tasks, you may find students need differing amounts of time to complete tasks. Being flexible with deadlines and helping students set individual goals for their work can help meet the unique needs of each student. * #### **Differentiate by resources:** Learning to use resources is a key goal of the course, and given resources provide an opportunity for students to self-differentiate in how they interact with key course content.
🔎 How does the curriculum support differentiation?

#### In order to meet the needs of a wide variety of learners, CS Discoveries is designed with flexibility that allows teachers to differentiate their instruction at the class and student level. Students are expected to take an active part in driving these learning experiences, seeking out appropriate levels of challenge and support with the guidance of a teacher. The course includes multiple features that allow teachers to differentiate instruction based on the levels and the needs of their students while ensuring that the class stays together and maintains a sense of community.

### Click here to read the full CS Discoveries Guide to Differentiation.

# Additional Ways to Support Student Learning Needs ## Can I print support resources for students who need paper copies of reference material? #### Yes, the text resources found in the "Help and Tips" tab of Code Studio are listed as resources in the "For the teachers" section of the teacher lesson plans. The resources are listed in the lesson plan where the concept is first introduced. Teachers can print these resources for students. Since these resources will be referenced throughout the unit, students may benefit from using a three pronged folder or binder to store their printed reference materials.
## Can my students have their lesson instructions read aloud? #### Yes, many lessons in Code.org's courses offer a read-aloud option for students. We recommend introducing all students to the read aloud feature as it can help students learning to read; students learning a second (or third!) language; and/or students who process information in different ways. Click here to learn more about read-aloud options.
## Do videos within the curriculum support closed captioning? #### Yes, all videos within the curriculum support closed captioning. After clicking the play button, click on the "CC" button in the lower right hand corner of the video screen to enable closed captions.

#### One way to support inclusion is to ensure all students have the opportunity to find success in their learning. Teachers can use differentiation to create opportunities for students to succeed in their goals for learning CS. Use the space below to document your plan for how you might differentiate learning opportunities for your students. 

### **Consider the following when making your plan:**

* #### What does my school require of me related to differentiation?
* #### When will I differentiate by task? 
* #### When will I differentiate by timing?
* #### When will I differentiate by resources?
dsls.title: Make a Plan: Differentiation

dsls.markdown:
# Assessment #### Your role as the teacher is to provide students with ongoing feedback in order to promote continuous growth.
🔎 How does the curriculum support assessment?

#### Frequent formal and informal assessment and feedback are critical to ensure that students are actively involved in their own learning and teachers have evidence that the class is making appropriate progress. We have incorporated opportunities for assessment and feedback throughout the curriculum to support you in measuring student growth and informing the pace of your instruction. However, since schools have diverse grading systems, it is up to you to decide how to use the assessment resources for grading purposes.

💻 Lesson-Level assessment

#### Opportunities for assessment within each lesson are listed out in the lesson plans themselves. These opportunities may include sections of the activity guides, discussion and reflection questions, or online programming and quick check levels. Guidance for how to use these opportunities to assess student progress is included in each lesson plan. ![Screenshot of an assessment level. A description box underneath reads, 'You can use this level as a formative assessment for students. Click inside the level to view a rubric and leave feedback for your students.'](https://images.code.org/75301032d5ad8904beefe6bdfb5524f2-image-1626709952308.51.41 AM.png)

✏️ Chapter- and Unit-Level Assessment

### End-of-Chapter projects #### End of chapter projects incorporate the skills and understandings students have developed in that chapter's lessons. These projects are designed to assess unit-specific skills and the five student practices that thread through the entire course. There is broad guidance for the activity, but project implementation leaves room for students to put their personal stamp on the creation. ### Post-Project tests #### Post-project tests are included at the end of every unit. These include several multiple choice and matching questions as well as open ended reflections on the final project of the unit. These tests are aligned to the learning framework of each unit and are designed to assess parts of the framework that may not have been covered by the project rubrics.

### Click here to read the full CS Discoveries Guide to Assessment.

# Assessment and Grading ## How do I assign grades in CS Discoveries? #### Although the curriculum does provide guidance on opportunities to assess whether or not a student has met a specific learning objective, it is up to the teacher to: * #### Determine the appropriateness of the assessments for their students. * #### Decide how to use, or not use, the suggested assessments for grading purposes. ## Consider the following: * #### What do I believe about the purpose of assessment and the relationship between assessment and grading? * #### What does my school require of me regarding assessment of student learning and grading? * #### What tools are available within the curriculum to support me in assessing student learning? * #### How will I assess student learning while implementing a lesson with students? * #### How will I assess student learning at the end of a chapter or unit?

# Task: Find Assessment Ideas on the Teacher Forum #### Once you have an idea of how you might like incorporate assessment into your CS Discoveries classroom, check out the teacher forum for additional ideas and resources.
#### **Step 1:** Open a new tab and log into your teacher account on Code.org. #### **Step 2:** Click on the "?" in the upper right hand corner of the screen and select the teacher forum option. #### **Step 3:** Click on the CS Discoveries category. #### **Step 4:** Click on the assessment category. (You will only be able to access this category if you are signed into a verified teacher account. Click here to learn how to become a verified teacher.) #### **Step 5:** Browse the posts to get ideas for how you might assess student learning in your CS Discoveries class.

#### Ongoing feedback is a crucial part of supporting student learning. Make a plan for how you will communicate your plan for assessment when you begin teaching CS Discoveries. 

### **Consider the following when making your plan:**

* #### Do I need to establish any routines associated with assessment at the start of the course? (i.e. students complete a daily exit ticket, there is opportunity for group discussion and sense making after watching videos within the curriculum, etc.) 
* #### Do I have to submit a class syllabus? If yes, what will I include about assessment?
* #### At a high level, what will I tell students, parents, or administrators when they ask how student learning will be assessed in my CS Discoveries class?
dsls.title: Make a Plan: Assessment

dsls.markdown:
# Connect with the CS Community #### Connecting with the CS community is can help students see the real world relevance of CS and consider how they fit into the broader CS community. It is also important for teachers to connect with other CS educators in order to learn and grow as professionals. ### **In this lesson, you will:** * #### Learn about opportunities to connect with the broader CS community.
#### **Suggested time:** 10 minutes

# CS Journeys #### Connecting what students are learning to the real world not only enables them to better understand and recall concepts but also excites and inspires them to continue learning. Code.org has created easy-to-use resources designed to help you illuminate real-world connections and give students opportunities to explore different career pathways. ## Why CS Journeys * #### Exposing students to different pathways and introducing them to diverse role models helps to broaden their aspirations and dispel stereotypes. * #### Real world connections bring learning to life. * #### Career exploration is fundamental to students' academic experience. ### Let's explore!

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
## Consider the following when watching this video:

#### How might CS Journeys help my students feel like they belong in CS?

dsls.markdown:
# Virtual Field Trips and Career Chats ## Virtual Field Trips #### Virtual field trips give students a firsthand look at the different ways that CS and tech are used in the real world. This is an opportunity to excite and inspire your students to keep learning CS!
## Career Chats with Professionals #### Career chats give your students the opportunity to hear from a diverse group of people using computer science to explore the universe, make cool robots and popular TV shows, and more! Students will learn about a number of ways they can use computer science to have a positive impact, as well as different journeys that people have taken to get to meaningful careers and achieve their goals.
--- ## Task: Explore Virtual Field Trips and Career Chats #### **Step 1:** Click here to navigate to the virtual field trips and career chats website. #### **Step 2:** Click to view the recordings of past career chats. Select one that might be interesting to your students and watch a few minutes of the chat.

![](https://images.code.org/4cf5a701b5a4f8e31575c015dc17fb66-image-1650470104370.54.54 AM.png) # Task: Explore Additional CS Journeys Resources
#### Consider the following as you explore: **How might I utilize CS Journeys to help my students feel like they belong in CS?**
#### **Step 1:** Click here to navigate to the full CS Journeys website. #### **Step 2:** Explore the additional resources and opportunities included in the CS Journeys program.

# Connect with Your Local Community

#### Another way to make CS come alive for your students is to involve your **local community**. Not only does involving your community in your classroom enhance the student learning experience by adding relevance to the curriculum, it also raises awareness about CS education in the broader community.

## Ideas for Connecting with Your Local community

* #### Invite a former student or guardian of a current student to come to class as a guest speaker. 
* #### Have students design a website or app prototype for a local business.
* #### Invite the person who runs the school website to class to provide feedback on student websites.
* #### Design an interactive card for a service member, residents of an elderly community, etc.
* #### Partner with a younger grade level to create games that younger students might use to practice skills they are learning.

## Make a Plan

#### What is one concrete step you will take to engage your local community in your CS class?

# Congratulations, you are finished with Module 4!
## Help us make this module better! #### Code.org values and is responsive to feedback. Please fill out this short survey to provide your feedback on this module. Take the Survey ___ ## Next Steps ### **Start Teaching** #### Check out this support article to learn how to create a class section. Once you've created a section, assign CS Discoveries to your class! ### **Continue Learning** #### Join us for a facilitator-led professional learning workshop. Click here to learn about opportunities in your area. ### **Revisit This Content** #### You can return to this professional learning at anytime to review your learning, access links or resources, or just refresh your memory! ### **Stay Connected** * #### Continue participating in the Code.org teacher forum. * #### Connect with @teachcode on Twitter. * #### Explore opportunities with the Amazon Future Engineer program. * #### Join the Computer Science Teachers Association (CSTA).
#### Thank you for learning with us!
